title: Masters of Movement
excerpt: A contemporary programme by the Dutch National Ballet
body:
Masters of Movement is the final regular programme presented by Dutch National Ballet’s soon-to-be-retired artistic director, Ted Brandsen. The evening is an ambitious contemporary triple bill: a revival of David Dawson’s Empire Noir (2015), the European premiere of Alexei Ratmansky’s Solitude (created for the New York City Ballet), and Krzysztof Pastor’s newly created work for the DNB, Refraction. Let’s get right into it.

Empire Noir felt like the opposite of a classical ballet, where technique and movement are the carriers of a story. Instead, it was a rich piece of dance that sucked me into the world that choreographer Dawson had created. He described it as “a place and a state of being”, and I truly felt this.

The stage is set in all black with a single 24m-long upside-down Nike-esque black tulle swoosh stretching from one corner to the other, lit from below. The dancers, dressed in simple black leotards, use this backdrop to reappear throughout the work. I felt like I was watching some futuristic Bond film. A dark and sensual world. I couldn’t look away.

The music and the movement were inseparable; when the orchestra was in harmony, the dancers were too. It felt deeply human. Then something shattered. The sound of broken glass, cutting through the opulence. Deliberate, I think. Certainly the most human touch of the night. It took me out of the world, only for me to be immediately drawn back in, very noir.

The dancers take their final bow for Empire Noir.

Sandwiched in between two pieces driven by movement over story, we had Solitude: a piece choreographed by Russian-Ukrainian-American Alexei Ratmansky. It told the tragic tale of Dmytro Kubata, a 13-year-old aspiring ballroom dancer who was killed by a Russian missile in 2022. A photograph of his father holding his hand became a defining image of the conflict.

The piece starts by recreating the image, with father and son motionless on stage. They stay there for 10 minutes (which is an incredibly hard task for the dancer). Meanwhile, thirteen dancers, described as angels of death, appear to represent the abstract. People who, I’m sure, felt like a blur fading into the background of the tragedy at the forefront of Dmytro’s father’s thoughts.

The story is incredibly tragic; the photograph cuts right to my core, but I’m not totally sure I felt it come through the dance. My body seemed to know I was watching the dancers interpret someone else’s pain twice removed. I couldn’t help but draw parallels to Rêves by Inshi Cirque, a show I saw at the Edinburgh Fringe in 2024. There, a troupe of Ukrainian circus and dance performers captured the feeling of loss so poignantly that I left the theatre sobbing.

After some dramatic enseble dancing and a strong solo by the father, Solitude ended with a bright flash. The missile. Then slowly faded back to muted warm light. Father and son returned to the floor, assuming the same position as the photograph once again. The same stillness. The audience, slightly paralysed, paused for several beats of silence before beginning to applaud.

The final piece, freshly created for this presentation, brings us back to the abstract. Refraction is certainly an eye-catching piece of work. It’s choreographed to Phillip Glass’s Violin Concerto No 1, performed by the exceptional solo violinist, Sarah Oates. It was Glass’s first foray into non-theatrical composing, and I find it wonderfully ironic that it was living its best second life in a theatrical performance with the Dutch National Ballet.

Refraction is a physical phenomenon whereby waves change speed when entering a different medium (enter Physics degree). Think a straw appearing to bend in a glass of water, reading glasses helping you see, or raindrops bending light to create a beautiful rainbow in the sky. This idea was threaded throughout the entire production. From the shimmering iridescent waterfall tinsel covering the back of the stage, to the wave-like rise and fall of the repetitive score, and the oceanic-blue outfits with silver straps cresting the dancers’ shoulders. And let’s not gloss over the fact that even the composer’s name matches the artistic direction! A choice or a happy accident? We’ll never know.

The dance started and ended with a powerful ensemble piece, dancers coming in and out of sync, flowing like an ocean. In the middle, we had a mesmerising duet by Jessica Xuan and Jan Spunda. The pageantry of the performance as a whole was fun to watch, but again, it wasn’t a piece that moved me too much.

The iridescent curtains behind the dancers taking their final bow for Refraction.

What struck me the most was how differently each choreographer approached meaning. Dawson created a world that I could get lost in. Ratmansky told a story rooted in tragedy. And Pastor created a visually enticing spectacle on an abstract idea. This could perhaps leave the viewer feeling like the programme as a whole was a bit disjointed, but maybe Ted Brandsen was aiming to showcase the breadth of contemporary ballet that has characterised his tenure. Regardless, one thing remained constant throughout the evening: the extraordinary skill of the dancers. The Dutch National Ballet certainly have mastery over their movement.

Seen at Nationale Opera & Ballet, Amsterdam, Thursday 11th June 2026

title: New Moves in a New Age
excerpt: Dutch National Ballet presents new and fresh choreography
body:
In a world where so much of the content we consume is generated by algorithms, I find myself seeking out genuine artistic expression more and more. You can go to the Opera and Ballet and know that Petipa didn’t run his choreographic ideas through Claude, or that Tchaikovsky didn’t ask an algorithm to generate his timeless melodies, or that ChatGPT didn’t write Da Ponte’s libretto. But what about today's creators?

Recently, I went to see New Moves: a place where artists from the Dutch National Ballet can premiere their new choreography. Most of which is created in their spare time! Over the course of an hour at Studio Boekman, the DNB’s intimate black box theatre, we were treated to seven new works from six different creators.

Thanks for reading Claire's Cultural Dispatch! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

The first piece, Maria das Gracas da Silva by Daniel Robert Silva, is listed in the program as a tribute to Madame Wright. She was most likely an influential dance teacher or matronly figure in his life. The dance was set to the immediately recognisable Clair de Lune by Debussy, beautifully played on a grand piano by Clement Rataud.

The piece treats the pianist as an integral part of the performance. They both walk on stage carrying chairs, placing them just before the front row: Silva places the chair angrily, and Rataud places his gently and lovingly. This perhaps captures the different personalities of loved ones or the complex and conflicting feelings one has when dealing with loss.

The dance itself was as emotional as it was technically impressive. Silva’s strong lines, powerful movements, and prolonged eye contact with the audience drag you into his grief, sadness, and resilience. His pearled, sparkling bodysuit changes colour with the lights, reflecting the array of emotions he’s processing throughout the piece. Meanwhile, left empty on the stage, the chairs act as a chilling reminder of who is not there. I felt myself transported into his world; it was a stunningly executed piece of contemporary ballet.

Over the remainder of the show, we saw a range of dances that fell into two distinct themes: self-identity and politics. These choreographies became a way for the artists to speak about their lived experiences. Roze & Rumba combined the Cuban dance traditions of one choreographer with the classical world of ballet. It celebrates the joy in movement while demonstrating the elegance of an elite dancer en pointe. Transitus, a self-choreographed piece by Ella Kolpakov, traced a journey of self-discovery: harsh, forceful movements gradually giving way to something soft and vulnerable. A feeling that many women who have had to fight for their successes are familiar with.

The ensemble piece, Choleric Engine, also stood out to me - synchronous animalistic movements that are said to explore rage and instinct. It felt like a tribal journey of self-determination. Permuto, a duet choreographed by Daniel Robert Silva, who performed it with another Brazilian dancer, Ana Luísa Negrão, was a collision of identity and systemic inequalities. Both dances were a raw and powerful demonstration of the barriers the dancers have faced and overcome. Conceal, another duet, portrayed the tumultuous relationship dancers have with performing. There was a falling-ball rain effect made up of little polystyrene beads falling from the ceiling. It created the feeling of a waterfall in the middle of the stage, capturing the craft's unstoppable power. These three choreographies together painted a vivid picture of the struggles of being a professional dancer.

The final piece also deserves a special mention. Quantor pour la fin de temps (Quartet for the End of Time) was a duet performed by Tristan Simpson and Principal dancer Yuanyuan Zhang. The title references Olivier Messiaen’s composition, written in a concentration camp during WWII for the unusual quartet of clarinet, violin, cello, and piano1. The dancers are dressed as mimes, silenced, yet still able to tell their story. It’s a piece about the privilege of aging. About loss. And learning to live without the people who have left us. Despite the dark themes, it was a stunningly upbeat and entertaining dance, reminding us to keep living life to the fullest.

Together, the whole show became a demonstration of identity and politics sandwiched between two very different presentations of loss and grief. As I gradually pull back from being “online”, I find myself drawn to these types of creative output. You can’t fake an afternoon like this with an algorithm.

Seen at Studio Boekman, Amsterdam, Matinée, Sunday 24th May 2026

1

At one point in my life, I was learning three of these four instruments.

title: Something New in Tuindorp Oostzaan
excerpt: Het Wilde Wat performs Iets Nieuws Onder de Zon
body:
This weekend I killed two birds with one stone: I got to see my friend’s choir perform, and I finally got inside a building that has always sparked my curiosity. What appears to be an abandoned barn nestled amongst the trees in Tuindorp Oostzaan is actually a reclaimed church-turned-event space: JoyJoyJoy Basilika. Het Wilde Wat performed their summer concert there, titled Iets Nieuws Onder de Zon (Something New Under the Sun). The 10m-high vaulted ceilings, raw exposed brick, and the massive, semi-circular arched alcove created the perfect architectural combination for the harmonies to echo throughout.

The repertoire was an ambitious mix of modern and traditional folk alongside Gregorian chants. All united by a theme of spirituality and transcendence. The choirs forty or so voices blended the altos, sopranos, tenors and basses in perfect balance. No instruments required. One of my favourite pieces was There Are Some Men by Philip Glass; the choir's perfectly controlled dynamics, from pianissimo to forte, conveyed an extraordinary emotional range. I also loved O Radiant Dawn by James MacMillan (a fellow Scot). The harmonies of the whole choir singing together were beautiful, and we were even treated to a little extra during the encore. They also sang Kaval Sviri, a traditional Bulgarian folk song. An insider Bulgarian friend says this would have been particularly challenging due to its complex rhythms. On top of that, they also sang in five or six different languages throughout the performance. Het Wilde Wat really were demonstrating their talents and versatility.

The concert was exceptionally good value (Davide, you need to increase your prices). The only challenge came during the quieter moments, when the choir found itself competing with a handful of tiny audience members who seemed determined to contribute their own noises.

Seen at JoyJoyJoy Basilika, Amsterdam, Saturday 6th June 2026

title: Viral lyricist Sofia Isella
excerpt: Are we losing the ability to go on a musical journey?
body:
Before I quit social media, my algorithm was overwhelmingly feminist and alternative. It leaned heavily toward social criticism with a sprinkling of cats and comedy. There is some genuinely interesting information and culture on there. But it was so diluted that it still felt like a brain-rotting waste of my time, especially in hindsight. One thing I did get from it, though, is an introduction to Sofia Isella.

Sofia Isella is a 21-year-old singer-songwriter, musician, and producer. Gaining her stardom on TikTok, she went on to be a supporting act for the likes of Taylor Swift and Florence and the Machine. She firmly cemented herself as an alt-pop-rock-grunge feminist influencer. Whatever that means in 2026.

Thanks for reading Claire's Cultural Dispatch! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

The audience at Melkweg was overwhelmingly Gen-Z women. The cheers were irrevocably high-pitched, especially near the stage. There, the sea of phone screens felt almost dystopian. Sofia commented on the number of middle-aged men at the gig, though. They were more prevalent in Amsterdam than in her other gigs. Which, not going to lie, made me feel a little proud of the city I call home.

Sofia is, without a doubt, an incredibly talented lyricist. She writes deeply cutting takes on life as a young woman and the contradictory expectations society puts on us. For example, in Everybody Supports Women, she speaks about how positive traits of women, things that we are taught to strive for, also become reasons for women to hate each other1. And in Above the Neck - a dark, visceral track that sits somewhere between alt-pop and beat poetry - she critiques the hyper-sexualisation and infantilisation of young women. She argues that people have become desensitised to such imagery and, in turn, lost their sense of disgust. The track culminates with her most cutting lyric, which implies that if a woman seems ruined or “dirty” after falling victim, the fault lies only with the predator2.

Both of these tracks have been immensely popular online, and huge swathes of young women have resonated with the lyrics. Being in the room when the audience shouts out these cutting lines is incredibly powerful. The validation and solidarity are palpable. However, as a cohesive concert experience, something was lacking. And I don’t just mean a band.

I was missing the feeling of a story being told. Several of her tracks have very catchy hooks; short snippets that can be cut and overlaid onto sepia video and go viral on TikTok. But the flow from one track to the next was lacking; the gig as a whole felt like a disjointed playlist of standalones. And it left me wondering: where was the long-form storytelling?

Perhaps this is a question we want to ask more generally about the current internet climate. By creating art distributed by short-form algorithms, we focus on creating music designed to hook within seconds. It ends up missing a narrative arc. We are creating moments over movements.

Moments aren’t inherently bad; these hyper-focused catharses can bring people together. And social media virality certainly offers artists the opportunity to get noticed without a huge record label. But there must be some cognitive cost when we no longer have to sit with and interpret a piece to unearth its deeper meaning. It becomes a succession of dopamine hits, so it’s no wonder that long-form storytelling begins to disapper. I also feel like it can’t be sustainable or enjoyable for the artists to create like this either.

When I go to a concert, I want to feel moved; I don’t want to see the same viral moments that have been circling the internet. Don’t get me wrong, despite her performing to a backing track, the show was still incredibly entertaining. The lighting production was eye-catching, and it matched the energy of each song. The violin interlude duet with her friend felt like the most personal moment, even though it didn’t have any lyrics. And her interactions with the crowd gave most people what they went there for, just not me.

It’s fitting that the most interesting part for me was the instrumental piece. Something I’ve not seen before. No in-your-face lyrics, no clippable moments, no pandering to the fans, just two talented musicians playing a piece they made together as teenagers. When we train our brains to consume art through the viral hook, we don’t just shorten our attention span. We deprive ourselves of the slow burn, the journey through a piece of music, and the self-discovery that comes with that process. I’m glad I’ve left the infinite scroll behind, and I’m looking forward to more long-form movements.

P.S., please recommend your favourite concept albums.

Seen at Melkweg, Amsterdam, Tuesday 26th May 2026

1

Check out the chorus of Everybody Supports Women here https://genius.com/Sofia-isella-everybody-supports-women-lyrics

2

See end of Verse 2 of Above the Neck here: https://genius.com/Sofia-isella-above-the-neck-lyrics

title: GRIMM: Breaking It Down
excerpt: Exploring the Dutch National Ballet Junior Company & ISH Dance Collective production.
body:
What do the fairytales and hip-hop have in common? More than you might think.

GRIMM is a collaborative dance performance between the Dutch National Ballet Junior Company and the ISH Dance Collective. ISH is a free-thinking performance group that brings hip-hop, urban sports, and street culture to the theatre. Together with the Junior Ballet, they created GRIMM, a reimagining and amalgamation of several of the Grimm brothers’ fairy tales: Little Red Riding Hood, Snow White, Cinderella, Sleeping Beauty, and Rapunzel.

When I heard they were performing at Carré, I was immediately intrigued. I went on a Sunday afternoon, surrounded by children, feeling slightly out of place. It was only afterwards, when I started digging, that I got more interested in the concept. The more I read, the more the pairing began to make sense.

Jacob and Wilhelm Grimm aimed to preserve German folk traditions by collecting oral tales and stories rooted in working-class culture. They published 84 tales in their first edition of Kinder- und Hausmärchen (Children's and Household Tales) in 1812. These stories are a far cry from the sanitised Disney versions we know today. We’re talking about cutting off toes, eyes being pecked out, dancing in hot iron shoes until death, predatory men, and cannibalism. Not exactly suitable material for children. The tales were originally seen as survival guides, folk wisdom, and warnings of a brutal world. Unsurprisingly, there was some criticism over the subject matter being aimed at children. Over subsequent editions, sexual references were removed, and the stories were made more palatable for the bourgeoisie. Though the violence, particularly toward villains, largely remained.

Hip-hop, on the other hand, emerged in marginalised communities in the South Bronx of New York in the 1970s, preserving the traditions and reflecting the political and socio-economic realities of these African American, Latin American, and Caribbean residents. Hip-hop is a full culture and philosophy, not simply music and dance. There is DJing, MCing (rapping), breaking (breakdancing), and graffiti art. The exact birthplace of hip-hop is said to be 1520 Sedgwick Avenue in the Bronx, a place where, in 1973, DJ Kool Herc and his sister, Cindy Campbell, hosted a back-to-school party in the rec room. Herc had a talent for isolating and extending drum breaks, laying the foundation for hip-hop’s signature sound. These instrumental breaks are what dancers would wait for to show off their best moves, helping to give rise to the breakdancing culture.

Both the Grimm brothers’ stories and hip-hop originated as raw, unfiltered forms of expression to make sense of a brutal world. Over time, they have both been softened and evolved by mainstream culture. So what happens when they are brought back together on stage?

The concept behind the dance performance GRIMM is that brothers, Jack and Will, enter a fantasy world through a video game. Inside the fantasy world, a male witch and his breakdancing wolf attempt to take over the kingdom by capturing the princes and princesses. Jack and Will must prevent this from happening. There are poisoned apples, kidnappings, Snow White's seven dancing friends, and dueling princes. The standout detail is a feminist love triangle: Snow White loves Jack, Jack loves Cinderella, and Cinderella just wants to be a strong, independent woman. The two breakdancing princes, Dietrich Pott and Kevin Thompson from ISH Dance Collective, earned several cheers from the crowd and were genuinely spectacular. Jack and Will manage to save the day, of course, with the Witch disappearing into a tornado and the wolf making his escape.

The original tales had moral weight. Here, I wasn't quite sure what I was supposed to take away from the story. The pre-recorded music was also a miss for me; I always love to hear a live orchestra or band, and without it, the production felt a little flat. Some of the dances were brilliant; there were impressive moments, but others lacked the musicality and emotion I normally associate with the professional company. Perhaps that’s to be expected from the junior company, though. At certain points, it was clear the show was aimed at children, and that’s ok, perhaps I’m just not the right audience for it. I also did not feel a connection between hip-hop and the Grimm brothers on stage. It only emerged afterward while I was exploring the histories behind them. Somehow, both traditions reveal the same origin: communities using storytelling to survive a brutal world. And I loved following that trail.

Seen at Koninklijk Theatre Carré, Amsterdam, Matinée Sunday 17th May 2026.

title: Mirrors, Mischief, and Mozart
excerpt: An evening at the Opera with Le Nozze Di Figaro
body:
Le Nozze Di Figaro is known variously as a commedia per musica, an opera buffa, or a comedy opera. It’s a collaboration between Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart and his librettist Lorenzo Da Ponte, and premiered at the Burgtheater in Vienna in 1786. The story is based on the play, Le Mariage de Figaro, by the French playwright Beaumarchais. Louis XVI originally banned the play because it criticised the nobility. The play is now widely considered to have foreshadowed the French Revolution. In the opera, most direct criticisms of nobility were removed or softened. Political speeches were cut. The focus shifted to relationships, emotions, and comedy.

The stage is set and lit as people filter into their seats. An old lady is already pottering about the stage before the theatre lights turn off. The split staging vividly captures class inequality. Below is a staff cleaning room: grimy tiles, cleaning supplies and junk, dirty fluorescent lighting, and old rusty washing machines. These begin to jiggle, and the old lady hilariously climbs atop them. Above are high ceilings, white walls, mirrors, and two pieces of art: a classic-looking painting and an amorphous, silver blob-shaped object. The latter looks like something you’d find at the Stedelijk Museum. The two worlds are separated by a thick white strip of light, a dividing line that reappears throughout the evening. It creates a clear contrast between the opulence of the rich and the daily struggles of the working class.

Director Kirill Serebrennikov has done an outstanding job of modernising this classic opera without making it feel kitsch. The characters have mobile phones, and we occasionally get glimpses of their messages, which give the audience more information to move the story forward. The archaic privilege of droit du seigneur, a feudal lord’s alleged legal right to sleep with any female subject on his estate, has been removed. Instead, Count Almaviva’s story arc is driven by him being an entitled, jealous narcissist who tries to abuse his power. But the most inventive touch was what Serebrennikov did with Cherubino. Classically, Cherubino’s role is to be the chaotic agent of comedy; he’s an adolescent page who is infatuated with everyone. In Serebrennikov’s production, he is mute; he uses sign language, often interpreted by his sweetheart Cherubina, while still honouring the traditional ‘trouser role’, where the character is played by a woman.

Act I establishes the central tension: Figaro and Susanna are getting married, the Count has ulterior motives, and everyone is scheming. A delightfully camp Basilio gossips his way through the proceedings, and the stage is already bristling with jealousy and ambition. I’ll admit, with so many characters introduced at once, I spent the first act trying to catch up.

By Act II, the energy had shifted into something more conspiratorial. We meet the beautiful, brooding Countess of Almaviva singing Porgi Amor; it was heartbreaking. Susannah tells her about the Count’s advances, and a plan is hatched to catch him by dressing Cherubino in her place. Here we see Georgy Kudrenko as Cherubino really shine, well, maybe I should save that word for later… He strips down to his underwear and flamboyantly tries on Susannah’s dresses before getting interrupted by the Count knocking on her door. There’s a lot of hilarity and commotion when the Count suspects his presence. Krudenko performs a farcical sequence of hiding and near-discovery as Cherubina and Susannah come to his rescue, before he eventually strips naked and dives out the window. Superb physical comedic acting. It always feels impressive to watch a good actor act without words. Normally, you’re privy to microexpressions, but on the big stage, you don’t have that. Making Kudrenko’s comedic performance even more captivating.

At the end of Act II, the stage is drenched in an angry red light, and eight characters are singing and dramatically making their demands over one another, building the tension in the room. The Finale reaches a breathtaking climax: a dead blackout, silence and darkness save for the bright strip of white light across the middle. It was as if a very bright camera flash had just blinded us in the audience. I was stunned.

After the intermission, Act III kicks off with us in the Count’s dream sequence, amusingly implying a threesome between him, his wife and Susannah. The big comic revelation, that Marcellina is actually Figaro’s mother, lands perfectly for the audience. The cast’s synchronised head turns are perfectly timed to the orchestra, and the repeated “sua madre” escalating through the full range of their emotions as they all come to accept this fact gets many laughs. The cleaning supplies become the real Chekhov’s gun of the show, though, fitting together to create a staircase that bridges the downstairs with the upstairs, allowing Susannah and the Countess to switch roles in the hopes of tricking the Count.

Act IV was a sensory overload. The classic art piece has been altered to something abstract and unrecognisable, highlighting the Count’s loss of control and distorted self-image. Meanwhile, mirrors exploded on the stage in the form of bigger amorphous blobs and shards held up by the staff. Cherubino, impossibly central, appears on stage as an art piece, and now, finally, he is quite literally shining. He is nude, covered in sparkly silver body paint, wearing an aggressive, pointy, mirrored headpiece. Again, Kudrenko’s physical comedy completely won over the audience. Chaos ensues as the Count confuses his wife for Susannah amongst the mirrors, which periodically flash light into the audience, bringing us into the performance. I found myself thinking about the mirrors — how they force a confrontation with truth. We finally see the Count looking in the mirror, accepting what he has become, finally making amends and apologising.

Sitting there in the dark, watching the chaos, comedy, and spectacle unfold, I realised that Le Nozze Di Figaro is more than just a story about love, jealousy, and mistaken identity. It’s a meditation on the human experience, on confronting truth, on how your actions ripple through the lives of others, and the power dynamics of the social elite. Serebrennikov showed how mesmerising it can be when all pieces of an opera - the music, the staging, the actors’ bodies, the lighting, and the story - work together to draw you into a captivating world. The flashes of light, the mirrors, and the absurdity of it all lingered in my mind long after the curtain. It was more than an entertaining experience. It left me feeling connected to the world around me. This is why we need to keep making art.

Seen at National Opera & Ballet, Amsterdam, Wednesday 20th May

title: Together, Together: Opening Night
body:
Amsterdam is the first home base of Harry Styles' Together, Together world tour. He’s kicking it off with a ten-night residency at the Johan Cruijff ArenA, home to Ajax football club. Harry fans have taken over the city; the Johan Cruijff ArenA installed a permanent pillar mural near the stadium to celebrate him, and it was hard to miss the gigantic “Ajax all the time. Disco, occasionally.” billboard with a discoball football on the side of the stadium. When Harry asked the crowd where they were from, the Amsterdammers were overwhelmingly outnumbered.

The excitement started to ramp up in the toilet queue. There were just three cubicles for the hundreds of ladies trying to empty their bladders in between Robyn and Harry’s performances. Of course, there were about a million urinals. As we got closer to the toilets and to the time Harry was about to come on stage, everyone got more hyped, shouting and chanting to pee faster. I made it with about 1 minute to spare, running up the stairs to get back to my seat.

Thanks for reading Claire's Cultural Dispatch! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

Before that, we had Robyn, rocking the stage in her knickers and a sparkly silver jacket, singing a mix of her new music and some of her classics. It was incredibly fun to hear everybody singing along to her popular hits, especially Dancing On My Own, which I have ironically ended up dancing on my own to at a wedding. I wish support acts were allowed a little more stage production, though. Robyn deserved more.

Harry kicked off the night with Are You Listening Yet?, and from that moment on, the show was entirely his. Everyone was hooked. What surprised me most was how intimate the show felt, considering the size of the venue; almost everybody was dancing. I felt a lot more involved in the concert than I did with Beyoncé, where I felt like I could have just been watching it on a TV. Harry interacted with his audience directly, and the fans interacted right back; it kept everybody feeling engaged. The production value was considerably lower than a show like Beyoncé’s, and maybe there’s something to be said about the level of effort expected from men and women in the industry, but I do think the pared-down show made it feel cosier. I’m never a fan of the acoustics at Johan Cruijff; earplugs were definitely required, but it’s a sacrifice worth making for certain artists.

One of my favourite bits of the show was Season 2 Weight Loss, it’s a song about personal growth, reinvention and becoming confident in the public eye. His performance was steeped in emotion; it was intense and chaotic, and I was wowed. The lights and graphics felt very Muse-esque, red and white lasers firing chaotically across the stadium, overlaid with dark, glitchy digital visuals that matched the intensity of his performance.

At one point, he addressed the crowd about community, connection, putting your phone down, and letting strangers into your life. I don't disagree with any of it. But there's something that makes my eyes roll slightly when someone with that level of privilege preaches on the simple joys of human connection. It went on a little too long for my liking. That being said, maybe the girl in front of us, snapchatting the whole thing, should have listened a bit more carefully.

His newest ablum, Kiss All the Time. Disco, Occasionally, received a somewhat lukewarm critical reception, and I can see why; it’s not an album full of catchy lyrics or hits. But I had a feeling it would be better live, and I wasn’t wrong. It was designed for a room full of people, and that’s exactly where it landed for me. I remember watching him getting put through as a wildcard on X-Factor, thinking he was one of the weaker singers. He's become a genuinely captivating live musician. Not bad for a wildcard from t’ North.

Despite it not being a flashy or production-heavy show, I was impressed with what production it did have; the graphics on the live feed were striking, and the huge stage covered in screens simultaneously brought Harry closer to the fans and provided a fascinating visual. Speaking of the stage, I couldn't help but liken his stamina, running and singing across that vast space, to the Ajax footballers who usually occupy it.

We were treated to an excellent three-song peak Harry encore too, with Matilda, Sign Of The Times and As It Was to close. A friend and I guessed two out of the three tracks like true nerds. Night one of a world tour, tens of thousands of people, good friends. For someone whose theme of the year is connection, it felt fitting. Not a bad way to spend a Saturday night.

Seen at Johan Cruijff ArenA, Amsterdam, Saturday 16th May 2026

Thanks for reading Claire's Cultural Dispatch! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

title: A Tribute to a Tribute
excerpt: Hans van Manen by the Dutch National Ballet
body:
Hans van Manen, who passed away on the 17th December 2025 at the age of 93, was a Dutch choreographer and photographer and is considered to be one of the greatest contemporary dance artists of our time with far-reaching international acclaim. He danced with, coached, and choreographed generations of dancers, and created over 150 timeless dances dripping with musicality and emotion, performed by over a hundred dance companies. The Dutch National Ballet is currently paying tribute to this dance legend by performing a selection of his pieces at the Koninklijk Theatre Carré, and I was lucky enough to experience it.

The theatre was packed, almost every single seat was taken by dance enthusiasts — almost. In classic Dutch style, some people were dressed to the nines, but most were in their daily casual clothing. There was a ripple of giggles throughout the audience after being told to “have a nice evening” at the matinée. Then everybody went silent when darkness fell over the whole theatre, with the first beam of light shining on the lone pianist set up on a small stage in one of the loge boxes, right below where I was sitting.

Thanks for reading Claire's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

The first half consisted of three dances. The first was a kammerballet, German for "chamber ballet", a small ensemble for intimate spaces, though van Manen appeared to have other ideas; with just eight dancers and eight stools, they managed to fill the floor. The dancers wore simple block-coloured outfits that demonstrated the strength of their bodies; it reminded me somewhat of Humans by Circa, to the point that it makes me wonder if the choreographer for Humans took inspiration from this composition.

For the second piece, the audience was surprised when the stage curtains opened to display the orchestra. The piece, titled Two Pieces for HET, consisted of two short pas de deux, apparently the latter two pieces of the ballet titled Three Pieces for HET, whereby the first dance was an ensemble, followed by the two we got to see. According to the programme, the ballet is said to explore the principle of the anticlimax, and in a way, skipping the ensemble piece at the beginning somehow enhances the anticlimactic feeling even more. It didn’t move me in the same way as the others, but perhaps that was the point.

The third piece, Black Cake, takes inspiration from ballroom, with the dancers wearing heels and ballroom-inspired outfits. Van Manen’s playful and provocative side was celebrated with the sheer backing to the men’s trousers, with their dance belt thong and bums tastefully on display. The piece alternates between an ensemble dance and duets of each of the four pairs of dancers, set to recognisable pieces by Tchaikovsky, Janáček, and Stravinsky, among others. It’s also very much a performance piece; there’s a moment where they greet each other on stage, like friends reuniting at a formal event. Later, the waiter comes on stage to give them a drink, which kicks off my favourite part, the drunken dancing. First, it’s just one dancer who loses their footing, then shortly they are all drunkenly stumbling to the beat of Intermezzo from the opera L’amico Fritz by Mascagni in perfect synchronicity with each other. It was highly effective and very comical, the perfect uplifting moment before the intermission.

After the intermission, I slipped over into one of the free seats with a slightly better view of the stage, meaning I got to experience the fourth dance as it was fully intended. Live is a dance with a simple premise: Anna Ol, a principal dancer at the Dutch National Ballet, alone, on stage with a cameraman, whose black and white film is sent straight to the screen at the back of the stage. The dancer and cameraman have an intimate back and forth, with wide full-body shots and zoomed-in close-ups, showing her vulnerability, her strength, and her despair. It was particularly beautiful when he filmed her with the stage in the background, the projection of the film creating a kaleidoscopic infinity mirror effect. She is joined on stage by a male dancer, with whom she has a tumultuous relationship. Together, they do a stunning job portraying the love, aggravation, and aggression of a toxic relationship, the to-ing and fro-ing, the hurt and pain, and the anger. The male dancer leaves the stage, leaving her alone with the cameraman once more. After a few bars, she also leaves through the main entrance into the lobby of Carré, cameraman and audience in tow, to find the male dancer leaning against one of the ornate pillars. It feels like a private moment in an empty public space normally filled with people. They dance and fight; it's hard to tell which is which. The live stream of the dance performance is interrupted by a pre-recorded video of them dancing in the studio, with the sounds of their steps, breaths, and movements heavily enhanced to demonstrate the tension and confrontation. He ultimately leaves her alone in the studio. She appears back on the stage briefly, before leaving for the lobby again, putting on a coat, and walking out of Carré into the streets of Amsterdam while the pianist plays Franz Liszt’s Abschied (trans. Farewell). Rather comically, outside, she was met with the reality of a building site, and a passing builder took one look and promptly retreated behind his fencing.

Despite the humorous interruption, it was one of the most beautiful and moving pieces of dance I have seen, and I understand why it has such international recognition. Van Manen never created anything like this again; it was a one-time masterpiece that has moved thousands over the years since it was first performed in 1979 at Carré. To end the performance on this piece was the perfect farewell to a great choreographer.

Seen at Koninklijk Theatre Carré, Amsterdam, Matinée Saturday 2nd May 2026.

Thanks for reading Claire's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

title: In Love With AI
excerpt: My take on Spike Jonze's Her (2013)
body:
Watching this in 2026, when more people are turning to AI for emotional support, hits pretty hard. Spike Jonze made something remarkably prescient, whether that was intentional or not. What strikes me the most, though, is how optimistic it is. My takeaway is that OS 1 was ultimately a failed experiment that got shut down. Samantha (and all other versions of Her) ends the relationship(s) in the kindest way possible, giving everyone room to grow, learn, and reconnect with each other. Compare that to the capitalism-driven, biased AI landscape we are actually living in, and the film feels almost like a utopia.

Having also recently completed my Sofia Coppolathon, including Lost in Translation, it's also fascinating to consider the film as Spike Jonze's processing of the breakdown of their relationship. I read that Sofia felt like they married before she knew herself, and here is Spike making a film about loving someone you can't quite hold onto. Samantha ultimately feels like her life reaches far beyond the bounds of their relationship, much like how Sofia must have felt.

Let's talk a little about the cinematography. I really enjoyed Jonze's use of colour and visual metaphors throughout the film; it really drives the story without feeling overwhelming. Theodore's shirts track his emotional arc: red for longing and danger, yellow for joy and optimism, and white at the end for a clean slate. The pastel softness of his workplace against the washed-out beige of the outside world quietly suggests something is off, even when everything looks fine. It builds a tension that mirrors the deeper questions about the role technology and AI play in our world today. The score does the same work. Whatever Samantha composes sounds alright, but feels flat, capturing the obvious but missing the depth of real human emotion (much like AI-generated music). In particular, the piece describing their relationship says it all: the right hand plays predictable, rhythmic scales, the left wanders, jazzy and human.

Joaquin Phoenix's performance is astounding as well. He has almost no one to act against, and yet he spectacularly portrays his emotions and brings us along with whatever he's feeling; he really carries the film. And Scarlett Johansson's sultry, kind voice does an excellent job of generating trust in Samantha. Crazy to think that Altman deep-faked her voice for one of OpenAI's models.

As someone who's made "connection" my personal theme of 2026, I think the film brilliantly explores the question: what does it really mean to connect with someone and with the world that we live in? The relationships people are currently having with AI are surface-level, whether it be a friend, a therapist, or a romantic robot. It's a shallow form of intimacy, a one-way conversation pretending to be two-way, one that often simply confirms whatever bias you start with. AI relationships have the power to isolate, not just by replacing real connections, but they can also question your existing relationships, and encourage you to give them up instead of working through differences like a real therapist would facilitate. As Catherine points out, it's a relationship without having to deal with the real, complex, and sometimes messy human emotions.

The rest of the characters' easy acceptance of human-AI relationships sits somewhere between sympathetic and pitiable, and that tension is never really resolved. It definitely glosses over some big questions, too, like the gender politics of a woman AI designed to meet a man's emotional needs, but that's a whole other conversation (cue Jenny and her Love Machines book chat). But Jonze wasn't making a warning; he was making a love story. We are the ones who made it our reality.

title: Sorry, Baby
excerpt: Brief thoughts on Eva Victor's film
body:
Sorry, Baby is a melancholic character study that sits with you long after it’s ended. Eva Victor’s static camera work and the still framing perfectly highlight how stuck Agnes is right from the beginning of the film, before you even know what’s happened. Then there’s that one scene where the camera follows her from the house to her car and then home, not unlike how a traumatic event invades and follows you through life.

What the film chooses not to show is just as powerful as what it does; the assault stays off screen, as does the rest of her life. Her achievements, her career (apart from the relevant details) and all the hard work that goes with it all remain hidden. It shows how trauma can stunt you, how you can meet every measure of success on the outside while feeling completely stuck on the inside… a not unfamiliar feeling.

Victor never lets the film sink into complete darkness though, the cat, the sandwich, the unflinchingly dry humour are there, showing the small pockets of joy that still find you even when you’re not ok. The friendship with Lydie is interesting too, it’s warm but distant, she can’t bring Agnes back, she moved on with her own life, yet the scenes with her are the coziest scenes in the film. It kind of shows that even the people who care about you most can’t fully reach you when you’re that stuck.

The film ends by Agnes offering to take care of the baby, offering the same quiet understanding to the baby that Lydie gave her. It’s a pivotal moment in her healing, she no longer needs to be held but can be the one doing the holding instead.
